You can absolutely achieve your goal without setting environment variables manually.
The Flask CLI framework actually provides a built-in mechanism for this specific scenario.
You do not need to rely on external environment variables to distinguish contexts.
The `flask` command itself triggers the `create_app` function in a specific way.
You can inspect the `cli` object to determine how the application was invoked.
This allows you to pass parameters directly through the command line interface.
A different configuration can be loaded based on the command being executed.
You might want to check the `ctx` object inside your CLI commands for clues.
The context object often contains information about the current invocation mode.
You can define a custom `create_app` function that accepts optional arguments.
These arguments can then be passed from the CLI command registration itself.
This approach keeps your code clean and avoids the need for shell variables.
Flask supports passing extra arguments to the factory function via the CLI.
You can use the `with_appcontext` decorator to access the app instance safely.
Inside your command, you can check if specific flags were provided by the user.
This logic can then trigger a different initialization path within your factory.
The server mode and CLI mode can share the same base factory function.
However, the factory can branch logic based on internal state or flags.
This means you can have distinct behaviors without separate factory functions.
You simply need to pass a flag or parameter when registering the command.
The Flask documentation suggests using the `invoke` method for such custom logic.
You can also modify the `app.cli` object to inject parameters before execution.
This solution is robust and follows the intended design patterns of Flask.
